sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on CSIRO, AgriFutures, Australian state agriculture departments, botanic gardens, peer-reviewed horticultural research, and the Rare Fruit Council of Australia archives.

core.intro: Finger lime is Australia's own rainforest citrus, and growing one is a little different from growing a lemon. The state sections below cover the climate, the districts where it grows, the harvest timing and the local pests where you are; the notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Every finger lime is the same species, Citrus australasica (long sold under its older name, Microcitrus australasica), so the choice is mostly about the colour and look of the fruit. Named selections are grown for a particular pearl colour: Durham's Emerald has near-black skin and bright green pearls, Rainforest Pearl and Pink Ice carry pink to crimson pearls, Red Champagne is red inside and out, Crystal is pale green, and Sunshine Yellow is a clear yellow. There are also hybrids bred by CSIRO from the native limes, which you may see alongside the true finger limes: the Blood Lime (sold as Red Centre Lime) is an acid mandarin crossed with a red finger lime, with blood-red skin and flesh; the Sunrise Lime is a small orange marmalade fruit; and the Outback Lime is a selection of the separate Desert Lime, not a finger lime at all. Pick the colour you want, then buy it as a named, grafted plant so it comes true (these cultivated limes are covered on our lime page).

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two trees?
sections.body: No. Finger limes, like almost all citrus, are self-fertile, so a single plant will set a crop on its own with no need for a second tree or a separate pollinator. A second plant or a little hand-pollination can lift fruit set, and helps a plant grown indoors or under cover where bees cannot reach the flowers, but it is never required. So if you have room for only one, a finger lime is an easy choice.

sections.heading: Planting, soil and light
sections.body: Finger lime grows wild as an understorey shrub in subtropical rainforest, and that shapes how you site it. It takes dappled light and actually prefers some afternoon shade in a hot, dry or exposed spot, though it sets the heaviest crops in good light, so the sweet spot is full morning sun with shelter from the fiercest afternoon heat rather than deep, all-day shade. Give it rich, free-draining soil at a slightly acidic pH of about 6 to 7; like all citrus it hates wet feet, so plant on a low mound if your ground is heavy and never let water pool around the trunk. Mulch well, keeping the mulch a hand-width back from the stem, and shelter young plants from frost and cold drying wind because they are tender when small (established plants take only light frost). The bush is densely thorny, with spines up to about 25 mm, so give it room. Container plants can go in through most of the year as long as you water them through summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Treat a finger lime like other citrus: keep the soil evenly moist, especially through flowering and fruit fill, and mulch to hold that moisture over summer. It is a light feeder by citrus standards. Give small amounts of a complete citrus fertiliser every couple of months through the warmer growing season, backed up with compost, worm castings or a seaweed tonic, and ease off in late autumn so soft new growth is not caught by winter cold. Keeping the soil slightly acidic (around pH 6 to 6.5) keeps the trace elements citrus need available, so a yellowing, hungry-looking plant usually wants feeding or a minor element rather than more water.

sections.heading: Harvest and the citrus caviar
sections.body: Finger limes flower in spring and into summer, and the fruit follows from autumn into winter, with some cultivars and warmer districts stretching the pick toward spring. Pick when the fruit is full and firm, has coloured up for its variety, and parts from the branch with only a gentle tug, because ripe fruit lets go easily and over-ripe fruit simply drops. Citrus do not sweeten or ripen further once picked, and a finger lime pulled green is sharp and disappointing, so leave them until they are ready. The reward is the caviar: cut the fruit across and squeeze or roll it, and the juice vesicles pop out as glistening pearls that burst on the tongue, prized as a fresh garnish by cooks and restaurants. They are best fresh but freeze well, so a glut keeps.

sections.heading: Pests: the gall wasp's original host
sections.body: The finger lime has an unusual claim to fame: it is the native host of the citrus gall wasp, the pest that raises woody galls on backyard lemons and oranges right across Australia. The wasp lived on the native limes before it spread onto cultivated citrus, and it will gall a finger lime too, so cut out the woody swellings in the new wood over winter, before the adults emerge in spring. There is a happy flip side, though: finger lime fruit is a very poor host for Mediterranean fruit fly and a recorded non-host for Queensland fruit fly, so unlike most citrus the fruit itself is rarely stung, which is a real advantage for a backyard tree. Watch instead for the usual citrus sap-suckers (scale, aphids, citrus leafminer and the bronze orange bug), and hand-pick or treat those rather than reaching for fruit-fly bait. Australia is also free of citrus canker and of huanglongbing (citrus greening), the diseases that plague citrus overseas, so a home grower here is spared them.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted finger lime rather than a seedling. Finger limes do not come true from seed, and a seedling is slow, variable and very late, taking many years (commonly cited as up to about fifteen) to fruit, while a grafted plant of a named selection fruits in about two to three years and gives you the colour you chose. Grafting onto a citrus rootstock (trifoliata or a citrange) also adds vigour and lets the plant cope with soils and climates the finger lime alone would struggle in. Check the label for the cultivar name. The in-stock table above shows the named finger limes for sale right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: How do you get the pearls out of a finger lime, and can you cook with them?
faqs.a: Cut the fruit across the middle and gently squeeze or roll it, and the little juice pearls pop straight out. They are used fresh, scattered over oysters, fish, salads, drinks and desserts for a burst of sharp lime, and are best added at the end rather than cooked, since heat dulls both the pop and the flavour. If you have a glut, freeze the whole fruit or the loose pearls and use them straight from frozen.

faqs.q: Can I grow a finger lime in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes, and it is a good way to grow one. A finger lime stays naturally shrubby and takes well to a large pot, which suits a courtyard or balcony and lets you move a tender young plant out of frost and cold wind in winter. Use a free-draining citrus mix, water through summer because pots dry out fast, feed regularly since nutrients wash out of containers, and pot up every couple of years. A grafted dwarf form stays the most compact.

faqs.q: Why is my finger lime so thorny, and how should I prune it?
faqs.a: Thorns are normal: the finger lime is a dense, spiny rainforest shrub, and young plants and vigorous new shoots throw the longest spines. The thorns do not affect the fruit. Wear thick gloves and long sleeves, prune lightly after harvest to open the bush up and keep it to a size you can pick, and you can even grow it as a prickly informal hedge. Avoid hard pruning in the coldest months.

faqs.q: My finger lime looks healthy but has never fruited, why?
faqs.a: The usual reason is age, not pollination, since a single plant is self-fertile. A seedling can take many years to begin, which is the main reason a grafted plant is worth paying for; a young grafted plant simply needs another season or two. The other common cause is too little light: a finger lime survives in deep shade but barely fruits there, so give it more sun, feed and water it steadily, and be patient.

NSW.intro: Growing finger limes in New South Wales. The northern NSW coast is the finger lime's native home, so this is about the easiest place in the country to grow one, in a backyard or on a small commercial plot.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The warm, humid, frost-free coast of northern NSW is the finger lime's natural climate, the lowland subtropical rainforest it evolved in, so trees there grow and crop with little fuss. Inland and on the cold Northern Tablelands the story changes: young plants are frost-tender, so in frosty districts grow a finger lime in a sheltered, sunny corner or a pot you can move under cover for winter.

sections.heading: Where finger limes grow in NSW
sections.body: The heartland is the Northern Rivers, the old Big Scrub rainforest country around Lismore, Byron and the Tweed, where the finger lime grows wild and where many of the named cultivars were first selected. Small commercial plantings run on down the coast and onto the mid north coast. This native provenance is why so much finger lime stock traces back to northern NSW in the first place.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: On the warm NSW coast finger limes ripen mainly from autumn into winter, with the pick often running from about March through May and some cultivars carrying on toward spring. Take the fruit as it colours up and loosens on the bush, not before.

sections.heading: Pests in NSW
sections.body: Citrus gall wasp is native to this corner of the country, northern NSW and the Queensland border ranges, where the finger lime is its original wild host, and it has since spread south through Sydney, so cut out the woody galls over winter before the adults emerge. Otherwise a coastal finger lime is fairly trouble-free: the fruit is not a Queensland fruit fly target, and the warm, humid air brings mostly cosmetic leaf and skin blemishes rather than anything serious.

faqs.q: Is the finger lime really a rainforest plant I can grow in shade on the NSW coast?
faqs.a: It is a rainforest understorey plant, so on the NSW coast it will happily take dappled light or a few hours of shade, especially relief from harsh afternoon sun. It simply crops more heavily with more light, so the best position is bright morning sun with some afternoon shelter rather than a dark, all-day-shaded corner.

faqs.q: Which finger lime should I plant on the NSW north coast?
faqs.a: Almost any will thrive there, so choose by the pearl colour you like, a pink Rainforest Pearl, a near-black Durham's Emerald, a red Red Champagne, and buy it grafted so it fruits soon and comes true to type. On the forgiving north coast the variety is about taste and looks, not survival.

QLD.intro: Growing finger limes in Queensland. The southeast corner shares the finger lime's native rainforest range with northern NSW, so it is both a backyard favourite and the base for some of the country's longest-running commercial plantings.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Southeast Queensland's warm, humid, frost-free climate is ideal, because it is the same border-ranges rainforest the finger lime comes from, so the plant feels at home and crops well with little coaxing. Heat and humidity suit it, though as a rainforest plant it still appreciates some afternoon shade in the most exposed, baking positions.

sections.heading: Where finger limes grow in QLD
sections.body: The native range reaches into the southeast, through the Scenic Rim and the Gold Coast and Sunshine Coast hinterlands. The longest-established commercial finger lime groves are here too: a well-known orchard at Bellthorpe, in the Sunshine Coast hinterland, has grown thousands of finger lime trees for more than twenty years. Plantings thin out as you move into the dry inland and the tropical far north, which is true lime country rather than finger lime country.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland's warmth gives a long season, with fruit colouring up from autumn and the pick running on through winter, earlier and more spread out in the warm subtropics than down south. As always, take the fruit only once it is ripe and parts easily from the bush.

sections.heading: Pests in QLD
sections.body: The citrus gall wasp is native to southeast Queensland, where the finger lime is its wild host, so expect galls in the new wood and prune them out over winter. The humid coast can spot the leaves and rind with cosmetic fungal blemish in a wet summer. The prize, though, is what you do not have to fight: as the core notes, the fruit is a recorded non-host for Queensland fruit fly.

faqs.q: Do I need to bait for fruit fly on a Queensland finger lime?
faqs.a: Generally no, which sets the finger lime apart from other backyard citrus in the state. Research has found the fruit is a non-host for Queensland fruit fly and a very poor host for Mediterranean fruit fly, so the pearls are seldom stung. Keep up normal garden hygiene, but you can largely skip the baiting and netting program a lemon or mandarin would need.

faqs.q: Can I grow a finger lime in the dry far west or tropical north of Queensland?
faqs.a: It is doable but harder than in the southeast. The finger lime wants the humid, sheltered conditions of its border-ranges home; in the dry inland it needs afternoon shade, wind shelter and steady water, and in the hot tropical north it grows but is outside its comfort zone. The southeast corner remains the natural fit.

WA.intro: Growing finger limes in Western Australia. Finger limes grow well in WA gardens and are increasingly planted commercially, but two things set the WA experience apart: the dry Mediterranean climate, and citrus biosecurity that shapes where you buy your plant.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Perth and the south west have hot, dry summers and mild winters, which is good citrus country, but the finger lime is a humid rainforest plant at heart. In WA it does best with morning sun and some afternoon shade, steady water and a thick mulch to carry it through the dry summer, and the warm, frost-free north grows it most easily of all. Full, all-day sun in a baking, unwatered spot is the quickest way to scorch and stress a young plant.

sections.heading: Where finger limes grow in WA
sections.body: WA's citrus belt runs from the Mediterranean districts north of Perth, around Gingin, Chittering and Bindoon, through the hills and the south west, up to the warm Gascoyne around Carnarvon. Finger lime is a newer, smaller crop here than the oranges and mandarins, grown in home gardens and a growing number of commercial plots as the species spreads west from its east-coast home.

sections.heading: Buying a finger lime in WA: the biosecurity rules
sections.body: Western Australia controls which live plants may cross the border through the WA Organism List and a Quarantine WA permit and conditions-of-entry system, and citrus is a high-biosecurity group, kept tight to protect the state from diseases such as citrus canker. In practice many eastern nurseries will not post citrus, finger limes included, into WA, so the surest path is to buy a WA-grown, certified plant from a local nursery. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA.

sections.heading: Pests in WA
sections.body: WA's main fruit pest is Mediterranean fruit fly, which is established statewide and shelters in citrus trees, but here the finger lime has an edge: its fruit is a very poor Medfly host, so the pearls are seldom stung even where Medfly is bad. Citrus gall wasp, whose wild host is the finger lime, has also become established across Perth's suburbs since about 2013, though not yet in WA's commercial orchards, so cut out any woody galls over winter.

faqs.q: Can I order a finger lime from an interstate nursery to WA?
faqs.a: Usually not. Citrus is tightly controlled at the WA border to keep diseases like citrus canker out, so most eastern nurseries will not send citrus, finger limes included, into the state, and bringing one in yourself can break the rules. Buy a certified, WA-grown finger lime from a local nursery instead; the table above only lists sellers that can supply WA.

faqs.q: Will a finger lime survive frost in the Perth Hills?
faqs.a: A mature finger lime shrugs off light frost, but a young one is tender, and the hills and cold inland valleys get cold enough to set new plants back. Grow a young tree in a pot you can pull under cover on frosty nights for its first winters, or plant it in the warmest, most sheltered corner against a sun-warmed wall, and it will toughen as it establishes.

VIC.intro: Growing finger limes in Victoria. Victoria sits at the cold edge for this subtropical plant, so for most Victorians a finger lime is a pot-and-shelter crop, but the state has a special place in the finger lime story.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Finger limes are frost-tender when young and want warmth that southern Victoria does not reliably give, so south of the Great Dividing Range they are usually grown in a large pot that can be moved into shelter for winter. The warm, dry inland Murray Valley is the one part of the state mild enough to grow one in the open ground, and even there a young plant wants a sheltered, sun-warmed spot. Citrus need no winter chilling, so a cold Victorian winter does nothing to help the crop and only risks frost damage.

sections.heading: Where Victoria fits in: the CSIRO native limes
sections.body: Victoria is where the finger lime was turned into modern fruit. At the CSIRO research station at Merbein, near Mildura in the state's north west, Dr Steve Sykes bred new lime varieties from the native limes, among them the Blood Lime (an acid mandarin crossed with a red finger lime), the Sunrise Lime and the Outback Lime, propagating them onto ordinary citrus rootstocks so the native-food industry could grow them reliably. Out in the open ground, the warm Murray Valley around Mildura and Robinvale is the Victorian district most able to grow finger lime.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: A finger lime in Victoria ripens late, from autumn into winter, and a potted plant brought under cover may hold its fruit a little longer. Pick only once the fruit has coloured and loosens easily; cold, dull weather slows ripening but does nothing to improve a fruit picked too soon.

sections.heading: Pests in VIC
sections.body: Victoria's cooler climate spares the finger lime much of the pest pressure of the warm coast, and the fruit is in any case a non-host for the Queensland fruit fly that the Murray Valley works hard to control. Citrus gall wasp has spread south into Victoria, so prune out any woody galls in winter. For a Victorian grower, frost rather than insects is the real worry.

faqs.q: Can a finger lime survive winter outdoors in Melbourne?
faqs.a: Only with help. Melbourne winters are cold and damp enough to harm a finger lime, especially a young one, so the reliable approach is a large pot kept in the sunniest, most sheltered spot and moved under a verandah or into a bright shed for the coldest weeks. Left in the open in a Melbourne garden it tends to sulk, where a potted plant you can shelter will crop.

faqs.q: Which finger lime is best for a cold Victorian garden?
faqs.a: Start with a grafted plant rather than a seedling, since grafting onto a hardy citrus rootstock helps it cope, and grow it in a pot you can move out of frost. The pearl colour is up to you, because in Victoria the deciding factor is shelter and a warm spot, not the variety: no finger lime is truly cold-hardy.
